name: Oklahoma
tagline: A strongly consolidated state IT model under OMES, a new statewide AI and modernization push, and a $9B Google-led data-center boom — Oklahoma is buying technology centrally and building AI infrastructure fast.

execSummary.summary: Oklahoma runs one of the most consolidated state IT models in the country: under the Information Technology Consolidation and Coordination Act (ITCCA), the State CIO at the Office of Management and Enterprise Services (OMES) controls planning, acquisition, and standards for technology — including AI — across 125+ executive agencies. That makes OMES the single most important account in the state. Three shifts matter for sellers in 2026. First, new leadership and strategy: Dan Cronin became State CIO in January 2025 and Tai Phan was named the state's first Chief AI & Technology Officer in November 2025, behind a FY2026–2028 IT Strategic Plan centered on cloud-native modernization and killing legacy systems. Second, AI and cybersecurity are now formal statewide programs, including AI-driven threat detection (Darktrace) and a published state AI-use standard. Third, a historic data-center buildout — led by Google's ~$9B Oklahoma investment plus Meta, IREN, and others — is turning the state into national AI infrastructure. Buying runs centrally through OMES Central Purchasing statewide contracts and cooperative vehicles (NASPO ValuePoint).
opportunities: OMES as a consolidated enterprise account — statewide refresh, cloud-native modernization, and legacy-system retirement across 125+ agencies under one CIO.
AI-infrastructure / 'AI factory' demand tied to the Google, Meta, and IREN data-center boom (PowerEdge, networking, services).
Governed, secure AI adoption under Oklahoma's published AI-use standard — private/on-prem AI for sensitive state workloads.
Cybersecurity modernization tied to the state's AI-driven threat-detection program and Cybersecurity Awareness initiatives.
Endpoint, client, and lifecycle refresh standardized through OMES statewide and mandatory statewide contracts.

stats.label: Executive Agencies
stats.note: IT consolidated under OMES / State CIO

budget.intro: Oklahoma budgets on an annual fiscal-year cycle (July 1 – June 30). The Legislature's general appropriations bill (SB 1177) provides roughly $12.8B for FY2027, a slight increase over the ~$12.6B FY2026 budget, with new dollars for education, mental health, human services, and Medicaid. There is no single published 'state IT budget' line — technology spend is centralized through OMES and distributed across agencies, then standardized via statewide contracts and enterprise agreements the State CIO negotiates.
notes: Figures are annual fiscal-year totals unless noted; Oklahoma does not budget biennially.
Most technology dollars are not a single line item — they flow through OMES central purchasing and agency budgets, so engage OMES to find the spend.

FY2027 (governor's proposed Feb 2, per NASBO): $8.12B general-revenue expenditure authority, +4.7% vs FY2026; $11.75B all funds (-6.7%). The enacted SB 1177 budget later landed at ~$12.8B; total reserves projected at $2.88B.

initiatives.name: OMES IT Consolidation (ITCCA)
initiatives.summary: Under the Information Technology Consolidation and Coordination Act, the State CIO at OMES owns planning, acquisition, standards, and implementation of technology — including AI — across 125+ executive agencies. The CIO can designate statewide and mandatory statewide contracts and negotiate enterprise/consolidation agreements. This makes OMES a single consolidated enterprise account rather than many fragmented agency buys.

initiatives.name: FY2026–2028 IT Strategic Plan
initiatives.summary: OMES and State CIO Dan Cronin published a three-year plan built on four goals: customer centricity, making complexity invisible, a future-ready workforce, and modernizing with purpose. The practical thrust is reducing service outages, retiring outdated/legacy systems, and moving to secure, cloud-native infrastructure and shared platforms — a clear lane for refresh, infrastructure, and services.

initiatives.name: Statewide AI Strategy & Standard
initiatives.summary: Oklahoma named Tai Phan as its first State Chief AI & Technology Officer (Nov 2025) and published a 'Use of AI in Oklahoma State Government' standard, fulfilling a recommendation of the Governor's Task Force on Emerging Technologies. Agencies need governed, auditable AI — an opening to lead with private/on-prem AI for sensitive data rather than open public models.

initiatives.name: Data-Center & AI-Infrastructure Boom
initiatives.summary: Google announced ~$9B to build a Stillwater campus and expand its Pryor facility for cloud and AI workloads. Meta broke ground on an AI-optimized Tulsa data center (Project Anthem), and IREN, Core Scientific, and Beale Infrastructure are adding capacity. This is the strongest tailwind for Dell AI-infrastructure and ISG demand in the state.

initiatives.name: AI-Driven Cybersecurity
initiatives.summary: OMES has stood up AI/ML-based threat detection (including Darktrace) and runs annual Cybersecurity Awareness initiatives under a recurring gubernatorial proclamation. Security is a standing statewide program under the CIO — an opening for secure compute, infrastructure, and managed services tied to the modernization plan.

procurement.intro: Procurement is centralized at OMES. Central Purchasing runs statewide acquisition under the Central Purchasing Act, and the State CIO has separate authority to designate IT contracts as statewide — including mandatory statewide contracts that agencies must use. Cooperative vehicles (notably NASPO ValuePoint via participating addenda) extend reach. The consolidated model means a few correctly-scoped statewide contracts can cover most of the executive branch.

vehicles.name: OMES Statewide Contracts (SW####)
vehicles.detail: Primary vehicle. CIO can designate IT contracts as statewide or mandatory statewide; agencies (and many locals) purchase off them without separate RFPs. Confirm Dell agreements and resellers are current and correctly scoped.

vehicles.name: NASPO ValuePoint (participating addenda)
vehicles.detail: Cooperative national contracts extended into Oklahoma via OMES participating addenda — e.g., the Carahsoft cloud-solutions addendum. A fast path for cloud and IT goods/services.

vehicles.name: Enterprise / consolidation agreements
vehicles.detail: The State CIO can negotiate enterprise and consolidation contracts across multiple agencies to cut acquisition cost — the lever for large standardized buys.

entities.name: OMES — Information Services & Central Purchasing
entities.role: Central IT authority and statewide procurement; the single most important account under the State CIO.

entities.name: Oklahoma Broadband Office (OBO)
entities.role: Administers ~$1.4B in federal broadband programs incl. $574M BEAD deployment in 2026.

entities.name: University of Oklahoma (OU) / Oklahoma State (OSU)
entities.role: Independent research-computing and AI-infrastructure buyers; OSU anchors the Stillwater data-center corridor.

entities.name: Health & human-services agencies
entities.role: Among the larger data-heavy agency IT spenders within the consolidated model.

procurement.competition: Oklahoma is a centralized, contract-driven market — winning means holding the right OMES statewide and cooperative agreements and aligning to the CIO's modernization plan. Incumbents and resellers (e.g., Carahsoft, WWT) hold cooperative coverage across compute, cloud, and services. Differentiation comes from cloud-native modernization fit, AI-infrastructure capability tied to the data-center boom, and governed-AI/security offerings rather than price alone.

powerStructure.name: Kevin Stitt
powerStructure.title: Governor
powerStructure.note: Term-limited; cannot run again. Open-seat 2026 race — GOP primary held June 16, 2026, with a likely Aug 25 runoff and Nov 3 general; expect cabinet/leadership turnover after Jan 2027. Pushes efficiency, AI adoption, fiscal stewardship; created the Task Force on Emerging Technologies and declares annual Cybersecurity Awareness Month.

powerStructure.name: Dan Cronin
powerStructure.title: State Chief Information Officer
powerStructure.note: Appointed Jan 6, 2025; 25+ yrs (Microsoft, Allstate, Marriott). Owns the consolidated IT model, statewide contracts, and the FY26–28 strategic plan; has assumed added oversight of cybersecurity operations. Named to Constellation Research's 2026 Business Transformation 150.

powerStructure.name: Tai Phan
powerStructure.title: State Chief AI & Technology Officer
powerStructure.note: First-ever role, named Nov 21, 2025; OU grad and former OMES CTO. Leads AI strategy and digital modernization across agencies.

powerStructure.name: Daniel Langley
powerStructure.title: State Chief Information Security Officer
powerStructure.note: Started ~Oct 2025; heads state IT security policy under CIO Cronin. Former Washington Technology Solutions deputy CISO and WA Lottery CISO; U.S. Army Signal Corps veteran. Leads Oklahoma Cyber Command operations.

powerStructure.name: Mark Wood
powerStructure.title: OMES Director
powerStructure.note: Appointed Dec 17, 2025 (former state Tax Commissioner, 37 yrs public accounting). Runs OMES administratively; reports to state COO David Ostrowe. Distinct from the State CIO/IT leadership, which sits under Cronin.

powerStructure.name: OMES Central Purchasing
powerStructure.title: Statewide procurement authority
powerStructure.note: Runs the Central Purchasing Act and statewide/cooperative IT contracts — the buying mechanism for the executive branch. Using AI process intelligence (Celonis) to police off-contract and overpaying spend.

news.title: Open-seat governor's race: GOP primary held June 16, 2026 (Stitt term-limited)
news.impact: A new administration arrives Jan 2027, with likely cabinet/COO turnover; CIO/AI/CISO posts at OMES are appointed roles that could shift. Build relationships now and watch for post-election leadership change.

news.title: IREN scales its 1.6 GW Oklahoma AI campus
news.impact: IREN's ~2,000-acre Oklahoma site (1.6 GW secured) plus May 2026 acquisitions (Mirantis, Nostrum) reinforce the state as AI-infrastructure terrain — tailwind for ISG/AI-factory compute and OU/OSU research-computing demand.

news.title: Meta breaks ground on AI-optimized Tulsa data center (Project Anthem)
news.impact: $1B+ Tulsa facility (targeted ~2028) adds to the AI-infrastructure wave; reinforces Oklahoma as a national AI hub.

news.title: Gov. Stitt signs FY2027 budget (SB 1177, ~$12.8B)
news.impact: Slightly larger budget funds agency services; technology flows centrally through OMES.

news.title: OMES leadership: Daniel Langley named State CISO (Oct); Mark Wood named OMES Director (Dec)
news.impact: New CISO under CIO Cronin runs Cyber Command/IT-security policy; new OMES Director (former Tax Commissioner) handles administration under the state COO. IT/CIO authority stays with Cronin — engage CISO on security buys.

news.title: Tai Phan named first State Chief AI & Technology Officer
news.impact: Formalizes statewide AI leadership; agencies need governed, auditable AI approaches.

dellActions: Treat OMES as the primary consolidated enterprise account — map Dell offerings to the FY26–28 IT Strategic Plan's cloud-native modernization and legacy-retirement goals across 125+ agencies.
Lead the AI-infrastructure conversation: tie Dell ISG / AI Factory offerings to the Google, Meta, and IREN data-center boom and to OU/OSU research-computing buyers.
Confirm Dell's OMES statewide and NASPO ValuePoint cooperative coverage and resellers are current and correctly scoped before pursuing agency deals.
Position governed, private/on-prem AI for sensitive workloads against Oklahoma's published state AI-use standard as agencies operationalize AI.
Engage the State CIO and Chief AI & Technology Officer directly — in a consolidated model, leadership alignment drives statewide standardization.
